oai_dc:dc.dc:title: An Undergraduate Experiment for Identifying Cotinine in Simulated Urine Using ELISA Screening and GC-MS Confirmation
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Zapien, Donald
Carley, Russell
oai_dc:dc.dc:subject: (-)-cotinine
urine
ELISA
gas chromatography mass spectrometry
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This experiment introduces undergraduate students to the distinction of and combination of presumptive and confirmatory testing in forensic analysis. The system tested was cotinine, the principal biomarker for nicotine use, in simulated urine. Students used competitive ELISA to indicate which samples presumptively contained cotinine. The samples that tested positive for cotinine were liquid liquid extracted followed by preparative TLC cleanup. The extracts were analyzed by GC MS confirming the identity of cotinine in the sample. The experiment taught the students that ELISA is a technique by which samples are screened quickly and that it is a presumptive technique only. The experiment used extraction and preparative TLC to reinforce the importance of sample cleanup, and that GC MS provided unique identification of cotinine, illustrating the confirmatory power of the technique. In this activity, students learned to distinguish between a presumptive test (ELISA) and a confirmatory test (GC MS). They recognized the limitations in the analytical approach. For example, though they expended time and effort cleaning up their sample, the complexity of the GC MS chromatogram can only be minimized, not eliminated. Students learned how to apply probability thresholds in their data, as exemplified in the determination of a statistically valid ELISA result.
oai_dc:dc.dc:publisher: The Council of Forensic Science Education
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: The Journal of Forensic Science Education; Vol. 8 No. 1 (2026): Journal of Forensic Science Education
oai_dc:dc.dc:rights: Copyright (c) 2026 Donald Zapien, Russell F. Carley
